coredata.dc:title: Experimental and modeling studies of N-doped carbon quantum dot nanofluids for heat transfer systems
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Meijun
Zou, Changjun
Tang, Wenyue
Huang, Yushuang
Sun, Huahong
coredata.dc:description: The thermal performance of the internal heat transfer fluid was an important factor affecting the heat transfer efficiency of the exchanger system. This study reports a nanofluid that achieves long-term stability without any additives. The synthesized environmentally friendly N-doped carbon quantum dots nanoparticles (N-CQDs) had a uniform particle size distribution, with particle sizes concentrated between 2 and 2.5 nm. The stability, thermophysical properties and rheological characteristics of N-CQDs nanofluids were investigated. The results showed that the Turbiscan stability index of N-CQDs nanofluid was about 1.5 and the ΔT value fluctuated within 0.5 %, showing excellent stability. The thermal conductivity of 0.1 vol% of N-CQDs nanofluid increased by 43.21 % at 50 °C compared to the base fluid. The specific heat capacity decreases with increasing nanofluid loading. The viscosity of N-CQDs nanofluid behaves as a Newtonian fluid. In addition, the models for estimating the viscosity and thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluids are proposed, respectively. Due to the different particle sizes and shapes of nanoparticles, the proposed model can better predict the thermal conductivity and viscosity of N-CQDs nanofluids compared with other empirical models.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot nanofluid
Stability
Thermal properties
Viscosity
Model fitting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic schematic diagram of N-CQDs nanoparticles.
(a) UV–Visible spectrum, fluorescence spectrum and excitation spectrum of N-CQDs nanoparticles; (b) emission spectrum of N-CQDs nanoparticles at 280–390 nm.
(a-c) HR-TEM images with different magnification; (d) particle size distribution of N-CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs. High resolution spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d).

Backscattering of nanofluids: (a) 0.005 vol% N-CQDs (b) 0.01 vol% N-CQDs (c) 0.05 vol% N-CQDs (d) 0.1 vol% N-CQDs and delta of backscattering: (e) 0.005 vol% N-CQDs (f) 0.01 vol% N-CQDs (g) 0.05 vol% N-CQDs (h) 0.1 vol% N-CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The TSI value of N-CQDs nanofluids over time.
(a) Thermal conductivity function curves of N-CQDs nanofluids with temperature changes; (b) The thermal conductivity enhancement function of N-CQDs nanofluids.
Experimental data and model thermal conductivity.
Comparison of the theoretical predictions with the experimental results of (a) 0.001 vol% (b) 0.005 vol% (c) 0.01 vol% (d) 0.05 vol% (e) 0.1 vol% N-CQDs nanofluid.
(a) Specific heat capacity of N-CQDs nanofluids as a function of temperature; (b) Variation of the specific heat capacity of nanofluids with different heating rates.
The viscosity curve of nanofluids with different volume concentrations under (a) different shear rates; (b) different temperatures.
Comparison of the theoretical predictions with the experimental results at different shear rates for (a) 0.001 vol% (b) 0.005 vol% (c) 0.01 vol% (d) 0.05 vol% (e) 0.1 vol% N-CQDs nanofluid.
Experimental relative viscosity and model predictions.
Thermal conductivity of standard liquids was measured by KD2 Pro at 30 °C.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: New information statement: This paper measures the stability, viscosity, and thermophysical properties of n N-doped carbon quantum dot nanofluids, and proposes new empirical models to predict their viscosity and thermal conductivity, respectively.

head.ce:title: Experimental and modeling studies of N-doped carbon quantum dot nanofluids for heat transfer systems

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CCDC Drilling Fluid Technology Service CO., LTD, Chengdu 610051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CCDC Drilling Fluid Technology Service CO., LTD

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CCDC Drilling Fluid Technology Service CO, LTD

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No. 8 Xindu Avenue, Xindu District, Chengdu 610500, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The thermal performance of the internal heat transfer fluid was an important factor affecting the heat transfer efficiency of the exchanger system. This study reports a nanofluid that achieves long-term stability without any additives. The synthesized environmentally friendly N-doped carbon quantum dots nanoparticles (N-CQDs) had a uniform particle size distribution, with particle sizes concentrated between 2 and 2.5 nm. The stability, thermophysical properties and rheological characteristics of N-CQDs nanofluids were investigated. The results showed that the Turbiscan stability index of N-CQDs nanofluid was about 1.5 and the ΔT value fluctuated within 0.5 %, showing excellent stability. The thermal conductivity of 0.1 vol% of N-CQDs nanofluid increased by 43.21 % at 50 °C compared to the base fluid. The specific heat capacity decreases with increasing nanofluid loading. The viscosity of N-CQDs nanofluid behaves as a Newtonian fluid. In addition, the models for estimating the viscosity and thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluids are proposed, respectively. Due to the different particle sizes and shapes of nanoparticles, the proposed model can better predict the thermal conductivity and viscosity of N-CQDs nanofluids compared with other empirical models.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the rapid increase of world energy demand has prompted the necessity to develop renewable energy. The main problem in the heat transfer system is low heat exchange efficiency, which increases energy consumption and costs. The heat exchanger transfers part of the heat of the hot fluid to the cold fluid, so it is of great significance to improve the heat exchange efficiency for heat exchange equipment. Generally, the widely used heat transfer medium in most heat transfer systems is water. However, in some cases, these heat transfer fluids cannot meet the high intensity heat transfer requirements [1,2]. Many explorations have been made by researchers to improve the heat transfer efficiency of heat transfer systems. The results show that the use of nanofluid as a working fluid can significantly improve the heat transfer efficiency of heat transfer systems [3–5]. Currently, researchers have done a lot of research on its preparation, stability, thermal properties and rheology [6–8]. In the last two decades, nanofluids and its applications have been intensively studied [9–12]. P. Selvan [13] uses CaCO3 nanoparticles as a stabilizer for SiO2/Ag nanofluids, in which the thermal conductivity is increased by 31.84 %. Kevin L. Main et al. [14] reported that the size of nanoparticles is the main determinant of ionic liquid nanofluids. Although nanoparticles improve the heat transfer performance of the base fluid, nanoparticles also increase its instability. Conventional nanoparticles added to the base fluid tend to precipitate, affecting the long-term serviceability of the nanofluid in the heat exchanger. The settling of nanoparticles also clogs the piping and increases the operating costs. In fact, if common metal and metal oxide nanoparticles exist in the environment for a long time, they will pose potential risks to environmental safety and human health [15]. Furthermore, the addition of surfactants to nanofluids can improve their long-term stability, but surfactants may damage the mechanical system. Therefore, the need to develop surfactant-free nanofluids with long-term stability remains challenging.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received more and more attention since they were proposed [16–19]. The preparation cost of CQDs is relatively low, and the particle size is <10 nm, which is a kind of green metal-free nanoparticle. CQDs have a wide range of raw materials [20–22]. Due to these advantages of CQDs, it shows enormous potential applications in the fields of biomedicine, drug release, and ion sensing [23,24]. According to different carbon sources, it can be divided into “top-down” methods (laser ablation, arc discharge, electrochemical synthesis, etc.) and “bottom-up” methods (template method, combustion method, microwave synthesis method, hydrothermal/solvothermal method, etc.). The raw material sources of CQDs are also extremely wide, and CQDs can even be extracted from biological waste and edible articles [25–28]. Ehsan-o-llah Ettefaghi et al. [29] used commercial ammonium hydrogen citrate as a precursor to prepare CQDs nanofluids, they discussed the convective heat transfer coefficient and thermal conductivity of the nanofluids. Albin Joseph et al. [30] used CQDs nanoparticles with 92 % solar energy absorption and 57.62 % higher collector efficiency than the base liquid. Fatemeh Seifikar et al. studied CQDs for solar energy harvesting. The experimental results showed that CQDs nanoparticles had an excellent light-to-heat conversion effect and could be used for multiple cycles [31]. However, the thermal conductivity of the high-concentration nanofluid was slightly reduced, which was caused by the small size of the CQDs nanofluid and agglomeration at high concentrations. Due to the low toxicity of CQDs, it is less harmful to the environment [32]. In addition, the CQDs are easily dispersed in the base fluid due to their hydroxyl carboxyl groups, and do not require dispersants and advanced equipment to maintain its long-term stability. Such good dispersion characteristics make it necessary to prepare stable CQDs-based nanofluids.

The N-CQDs nanofluid proposed in this study can maintain long-term stability without additives. At present, researchers do not have much research and analysis on the heat transfer performance of CQDs nanofluids. Only a few articles have explored the heat transfer properties of CQDs nanofluids. In this study, not only the thermophysical properties of N-CQDs nanofluids were explored. The rheological behavior and stability of the nanofluid was also studied to explain why it does not cause severe blockages in the system. Accurate thermal conductivity can be obtained by experimental measurement, but it was expensive. Therefore, this study proposes new models to help predict the thermal conductivity and viscosity of N-CQDs nanofluids. By comparing the proposed empirical model with other models, the new empirical model can better predict the thermal conductivity with temperature and viscosity with the concentration of N-CQDS nanofluids.

As shown in Fig. 1 , 6.4 g of citric acid, 2 g of urea and 40 mL of deionized water were prepared as a clear solution and then loaded into a 50 mL autoclave and heated for 5 h at 200 °C. The samples were purified with a 3000 molecular weight dialysis membrane for 24 h with water changes every 3 h. Finally, the purified sample of 10 mL N-CQDs was freeze-dried and the concentration (1.2 mg/mL) was determined. N-CQDs nanofluids with volume fractions of 0.001 vol%, 0.005 vol%, 0.01 vol%, 0.05 vol%, and 0.1 vol% were prepared using the samples. In the process of preparing N-CQDs nanofluid, pure water is mainly used as the cooling medium (base fluids). A certain amount of N-CQDs solution and pure water were mixed into beakers, and then sonicated for 1 h to improve the dispersion stability of the N-CQDs nanofluids.

The infrared interference light absorption of N-CQDs nanoparticles was measured by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Beijing Ruili Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., WQF-520). The UV–Visible absorption spectrum was obtained by using a UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan, UV-180). The fluorescence spectrum was obtained by a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi High-Tech Corporation, F-7100). The morphology and lattice spacing of N-CQDs nanoparticles were photographed and analyzed using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (FEI Company, FEI Talos F200S) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) (American Brookhaven Instruments, BI-200SM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermoscientific NEXSA, AI Ka radiation) was using to analyze the surface composition and chemical state.

The Turbiscan stability analyzer (TLAB) (Formulaction, France) was used to characterize the scattered light of nanofluids with different volume concentrations over time. The optical probe scans every 30 s with the height of the sample (up to 55 mm), and transmitted and backscattering light data were collected every 40 μm height. In this study, because the N-CQDs nanofluid has a good light permeability, the stability state of N-CQDs nanofluids over time can be analyzed by the transmitted light (T) curve. T is calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
T =
T 0
exp
− 3
r i
ϕ
Q e
d
d
where T 0 is the continuous phase transmittance; r
i is the inner radius of the bottle; d is the nanoparticle size; ϕ is the volume fraction of nanofluid; Q
e is the parameters of Mie Theory.

The average transmission intensity change (ΔT) can be calculated as Eqs. (2) and (3):
(2)
T i
=
∑
H f
H b
Scan i
h
−
Scan 0
h
H b
−
H f
(3)
Δ T =
T i
−
T 0
where T
i and T 0 are the transmitted light intensity at time 0 and time i, respectively; H
b is the highest liquid level; H
f is the lowest liquid level; Scan
i (h) and Scan 0(h) are the scanning light intensity at height h and time i and time 0 respectively.

The KD2 Pro Thermal Properties Analyzer (Decagon Devices, USA) measured the thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluids at different temperatures. The KD2 Pro used the linear heat source method and had a measurement accuracy of ±5 %. To confirm the performance of the sensor, glycerol was measured at a temperature of 30 °C. Ensure the target temperature for all experiments by using a thermostatic water bath. And by calculating the average of 3 measurements as the value of thermal conductivity for each temperature. The temperature during heating of the nanofluid is expressed by Eq. (4):
(4)
T =
t 0
+
m b
t +
m f
ln t
where t 0 represents the ambient temperature during heating; t is the heating time of the probe; m
b is the rate of background temperature drift; m
f is the slope of temperature rise and the logarithm of temperature.

The temperature during cooling of the nanofluid is expressed by Eq. (5):
(5)
T =
t 0
+
m b
t +
m f
ln
t
t −
t i
where i is the number of cooling cycles.

To determine the increase in thermal conductivity expressed as a percentage, Eq. (6) is used:
(6)
En =
k f
−
k b
k b
× 100 %
where k is the thermal conductivity, and the subscripts f and b indicate nanofluid and base fluid.

To estimate the deviation of the measured thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluid, the experimental setup was kept at a constant temperature of 30 °C for five measurements on pure water and ethylene glycol standard solutions, respectively. The results showed that the error range was within ±2 %, as shown in Table 1 .

The specific heat capacity of nanofluids at different temperature rates was measured by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). In the experiment, about 5 mg of N-CQDs nanofluids with different volume concentrations were weighed, the heating rate was 10 °C/min, the starting temperature was 30 °C, and the final temperature was 80 °C, and then kept at a constant temperature for 5 min. The specific heats of nanofluids with a volume fraction of 0.1 vol% were measured at 20 °C/min and 5 °C/min, respectively.

The viscosity of the N-CQDs nanofluid was obtained by a rheometer (HAAKE, Germany) with shear rates varying continuously from 100 s−1 to 800 s−1. Each nanofluid was tested for 10 min and 100 continuous readings were obtained. To confirm the accuracy of the measuring instrument, use pure water to calibrate at 20 °C.

The FT-IR spectrum of the N-CQDs particles is shown in Fig. 2 . The absorption peak between 3206 cm−1 and 3435 cm−1 originates from NH and OH vibrations. The stretching of 1585 cm−1 and 1716 cm−1 corresponds to the CO and CO stretching vibration. The carbonyl group (CC) stretches at 1407 cm−1, the stretch at 1190 cm−1 is the CN bond, and the peak at 1115 cm−1 represents the CO bond.

Fig. 3 (a) presents the UV–Visible spectrum, fluorescence spectrum and excitation spectrum of N-CQDs particles. The distinct absorption peak at 238 nm is due to a π-π* jump in the conjugated double bond CC, and 363 nm is attributed to the n-π* leap in CO. The fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs in the wavelength range of 280–390 nm are shown in Fig. 3 (b). The results show that 439 nm is the peak of the emission spectrum of N-CQDs, while 376 nm is the excitation light wavelength. In addition, the observed excitation independence (280–390 nm) is due to the presence of surface state defects in the N-CQD, which are also usually associated with blue emission. This is also consistent with the previously reported phenomenon of doped CQDs [33].

In Fig. 4 , the spherical shape of the N-CQDs nanoparticles in the HR-TEM image. In addition, the particle size of the carbon quantum dots is uniformly distributed (2.0 to 2.5 nm). The HR-TEM images show a lattice spacing of 0.28 nm as shown in Fig. 4 (c).

The chemical structure of N-CDs was further characterized by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs exhibited three characteristic peaks at 288.05 eV, 400.08 eV, and 531.05eV for C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. 5 (b), the C1s spectrum is classified into four components appeared at 288.03 eV, 285.98 eV, 284.50 eV and 283.93 eV, which are associated with CO, CO, CN and CC bonds, respectively. The formation of CN bond implies that N atoms are efficiently doped into CQDs. The spectrum of N1s peak in Fig. 5 (c) at 399.58 eV and 398.33 eV, corresponding to CH and CNC, respectively. As manifested in Fig. 5 (d), the peaks of O1s spectrum at 532.48 eV and 531.28 eV are indexed to CO and CO, respectively. Based on the above results, XPS analyses demonstrate the co-existence of H, C, N and O elements in the N-CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the blue line represents the first scan result, and the red line represents the last scan result. It can be seen that the T value distribution curve of N-CQDs nanofluids with different volume concentrations does not change significantly with the increase of measurement time. With the increase in the volume concentration of N-CQDs nanofluids, the decrease of T value is because the increase of nanofluid concentration leads to the agglomeration of nanoparticles and the decrease of light transmittance. The absolute value of light intensity in the middle part is related to the particle size and concentration of the nanoparticles. The empirical value of ΔT value in the middle part of the sample fluctuates within 0.5 %, indicating its excellent stability. The ΔT value fluctuated slightly at the bottom and the top of the sample, which is due to the agglomeration between the N-CQDs particles and then floating at the bottom. This shows more excellent stability compared to some previous studies on nanofluids [34–37].

The Turbiscan stability index (TSI) value is the accumulation of T value changes between each measurement and the last measurement, reflecting the changes in the concentration and particle size of the entire sample [38]. The smaller the TSI value, the more stable the measured system. TSI is calculated by Eq. (7):
(7)
TSI =
∑ n
∑ h
scan n
−
scan
n − 1
H
where n is the number of measurements; h is the scanning height of the instrument; H is the measure the maximum height.

As shown in Fig. 7 , the overall TSI values in the nanofluids with different volume fractions did not vary significantly and the TSI values were all low. It is shown that the stability of N-CQDs nanofluid hardly affects its stability as the volume fraction increases. N-CQDs nanofluids exhibit excellent stability at the macroscopic level and are suitable for use as working fluids in heat exchangers.

The thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluids in the volume fraction range of 0.001–0.1 % and at temperatures of 30–50 °C is shown in Fig. 8 . The thermal conductivity increases as the temperature rises. Thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluids with 0.001 vol%, 0.005 vol%, 0.01 vol%, 0.05 vol%, and 0.1 vol% increased by 2.25 %, 4.68 %, 5.98 %, 7.28 %, and 10.35 %, respectively, at 30 °C when compared to the base fluid. The thermal conductivity increased by 12.31 %, 21.58 %, 24.05 %, 34.09 %, and 43.21 % at a temperature of 50 °C, respectively. The Brownian motion between the nanoparticles causes an increase in the thermal conductivity of the nanoparticles themselves as the temperature rises. Furthermore, when the number of nanoparticles added rises, the heat conductivity increases. Because the increase of nanoparticle concentration facilitates the collision between nanoparticles, and N-CQDs are small-sized nanoparticles, which are more conducive to the heat transfer between nanoparticles and fluid. The results show that adding nanoparticles and raising the temperature may significantly increase the thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluid.

Many researchers have presented models to predict the thermal conductivity of nanofluids in the past, and these models have all been created to predict the thermal conductivity of nanofluids. Abdul Hamid et al. [39] proposed a model for predicting the thermal conductivity of TiO2 nanofluids:
(8)
K nf
K f
=
1 + ϕ
7
T 80
0.024

Sunder et al. [40–41] proposed an empirical model for measuring the thermal conductivity of Nanodiamond and Fe3O4-water nanofluids:
(9)
K nf
K f
= 1.063
1 + ϕ
0.122
T 60
0.048
(10)
K nf
K f
=
1 + 10.5 ϕ
0.1051

Abdolbaqi et al. [42] proposed a model for measuring the thermal conductivity of Bioglycol/water based TiO2 nanofluids:
(11)
K nf
K f
= 1.308
ϕ 0.042
T 80
0.011

At present, the thermal conductivity model of N-CQDs nanofluids has not been proposed by researchers. By nonlinear curve fitting of the experimental data, the following Eq. (12) is produced, which is utilized to estimate the thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluids based on the correlation between temperature and nanofluid volume fraction. The thermal conductivity calculated by the predicted model is generally consistent with the experimental results, as shown in Fig. 9 .
(12)
K
n f
−
K f
K f
= 1.458
T 60
3.5
ϕ 0.26
+ 1.7 ×
10
− 3
where the average error is: R 2 = 0.98675, K nf is the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid and K
f is the thermal conductivity of the base fluid.

As shown in Fig. 10 , to test the accuracy of the model proposed in this study, the models proposed in some previous studies are used to compare the thermal conductivity predicted by the model in this study. The results show that these models cannot accurately predict the thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluids at different temperatures. Because these models are not developed for N-CQDs nanofluids, it is normal that the thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluids cannot be predicted. The thermal conductivity of nanofluids is related to the particle size, quantity, and shape of nanoparticles, so few models can be used to predict the thermal conductivity of all nanofluids. The model proposed in this study can better capture the trend of thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluids.

Adding nanoparticles to the base fluid reduces the specific heat, as shown in Fig. 11 (a). At a temperature of 80 °C, the specific heat capacity of 0.1 vol% nanofluid is 3.249 KJ/(kg·K). This is because water has a larger specific calorific value than nanomaterials. The specific heat of N-CQDs nanofluids is investigated at various heating speeds, and the results are shown in Fig. 11(b). At varying heating speeds, the specific heat capacities of N-CQDs nanofluids varied substantially. When the heating rate of N-CQDs nanofluids increases, the specific heat also increases.

Fig. 12 (a) shows the relational function of N-CQDs nanofluids viscosity at different shear rates. The viscosity of the nanofluid fluctuates slightly with the change of shear rate. It shows that the nanofluid behaves as a Newtonian fluid in the test range of shear rate 100 to 800 s−1. The viscosity of the nanofluid increases with the increase of N-CQDs nanoparticle loading, which is inevitable. Fig. 12 (b) shows the viscosity of the N-CQDs nanofluid at different temperatures, which decreases with increasing temperature. The main reason is Brownian motion, where the collision frequency and kinetic energy of nanoparticles increase with increasing temperature and spend less time in contact with each other, reducing the probability of nanoparticles agglomeration. N-CQDs nanoparticles are characterized by high stability, small particle size, and small change in viscosity, which reduces the harm of nanofluids to mechanical systems.

The first equation is the Einstein model, as shown in Eq. (13) [43]. This model is suitable for the case where the particle volume fraction is small (<1 %):
(13)
μ nf
/
μ f
= 2.5
ϕ vol
+ 1
where m and ρ are the mass and density, respectively; subscripts np and bf are nanofluid and base fluid, respectively.

Later, Brinkman [44] extended Einstein's equation to Eq. (14), which applies to the case of particle volume fraction (<4 %):
(14)
μ nf
/ μ =
1 −
ϕ vol
− 2.5

In addition, the Krieger-Dougherty model [45] is considered to be an effective model for the viscosity of a higher volume fraction of nanofluids, which can be expressed by Eq. (15):
(15)
μ nf
/
μ f
=
1 −
ϕ vol
ϕ m
−
η
ϕ m
where [η] is the intrinsic viscosity ([η] = 2.5), and ϕ m (ϕ m ≈ 0.65) is the maximum volume fraction.

Another model is the improved Maron-Pierce model, which takes into account the Brownian motion of suspended nanoparticles and the collisions between them [46], as shown in Eq. (16):
(16)
μ nf
/
μ f
=
1 −
ϕ vol
ϕ m
− 2

For the viscosity of nanofluids, Wang et al. developed the following model, which may be represented by Eq. (17):
(17)
μ nf
/
μ f
= 1 + 7.3
ϕ vol
+ 123
ϕ vol 2

The relative viscosity of N-CQDs nanofluids was compared to the traditional viscosity model at 25 °C, as shown in Fig. 13 . These five models generally predict the viscosity of N-CQD nanofluids well at low concentrations. But as the volume fraction of nanoparticles increases, the model significantly underestimates the viscosity of the nanofluid. It is also reasonable that these models do not apply to N-CQDs nanofluids due to the small size of N-CQDs nanoparticles and the increased viscosity due to Brownian motion and aggregation of particles. The viscosity of N-CQDs nanofluids may be estimated using the prediction model (18) derived by curve fitting the experimental data. As seen in Fig. 14 , the experimentally determined relative viscosity is very similar to the data predicted by the model.
(18)
μ nf
=
μ f
1 + 5.4863
ϕ vol 0.66
where the average error is: R2 = 0.99826.

The main work of this paper includes the study of the stability, specific heat capacity, thermal conductivity, and viscosity of N-CQDs nanofluids, as well as the proposed models for predicting the thermal conductivity and viscosity of N-CQDs nanofluids, respectively. Conclusions that can be reached include the following:
(1)
In the TLAB test, N-CQDs nanofluid did not settle or float, and the overall TSI values were small, indicating its high stability.
(2)
The thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluid was shown to be positively connected with nanoparticle volume fraction and to vary significantly with temperature. Compared with the base fluid, the thermal conductivity of the nanofluid at 50 °C is increased by 43.21 %. The thermal conductivity of N-CQDs nanofluids is better predicted by the empirical model presented in this paper:
K
n f
−
K f
K f
= 1.458
T 60
3.5
ϕ 0.26
+ 1.7 ×
10
− 3
.
(3)
The specific heat capacity of N-CQDs nanofluids decreases with increasing volume concentration, while the specific heat capacity increases with the increase of the heating rate.
(4)
The N-CQDs nanofluid exhibits Newtonian fluid behavior with increasing shear rate. Furthermore, an empirical correlation between the viscosity and volume fraction of N-CQDs nanofluids was proposed as follows: μ
nf = μ
f (1 + 5.4863ϕ
vol
0.66).

In conclusion, N-CQDs nanofluid had excellent stability, high thermal conductivity, and excellent viscosity. The whole preparation process has low investment, which can reduce the waste of electricity and heat in the process of use. N-CQDs nanofluids had the potential to be used in heat exchange systems. However, how to mass-produce N-CQDs nanofluids so that it can be better used in industry is the main problem to be solved in the future.

Meijun Chen: Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Changjun Zou: Funding acquisition, Resources. Wenyue Tang: Validation. Yushuang Huang: Supervision. Huahong Sun: Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21576225), Sichuan Province Science and Technology Support Program (2019YFG0054).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of hybrid-nanofluid in heat transfer enhancement – a review
